coredata.dc:title: Preparation of pH sensitive P-doped carbon dots-loaded starch/polyvinyl alcohol packaging film for real-time monitoring freshness of pork

coredata.dc:description: Using biodegradable films as smart packaging to monitor the freshness of meat in real time is a promising and challenging work. Herein, the phosphorus-doped carbon dots (P-CDs) with pH sensitivity were coated on starch/polyvinyl alcohol film (SP film) by a simple scrape-coating method and applied to monitor the freshness of pork. The P-CDs@SP film exhibits a uniform surface, excellent mechanical properties, higher light blocking. When the total volatile base nitrogen (TVB-N) of pork exceeded 15 mg/100 g, the color of the film changed from brownish-yellow to purple-red, and the total color difference (ΔE) of the film obviously increased from 10.24 to 9.37 to 20.75 and 22.93 under the storage conditions of 25 and 4 °C, respectively. This can be attributed to the abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of P-CDs and SP film to promote their stable binding through hydrogen bonds, which greatly improves the hydrophilicity of the films, providing conditions for the rapid conversion of the TVB-N to ammonia and the formation of an alkaline environment. This work can simply and effectively monitor the freshness of pork through the changes of film color, will provide a new idea for the preparation of biodegradable smart packaging materials.

head.ce:title: Preparation of pH sensitive P-doped carbon dots-loaded starch/polyvinyl alcohol packaging film for real-time monitoring freshness of pork

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using biodegradable films as smart packaging to monitor the freshness of meat in real time is a promising and challenging work. Herein, the phosphorus-doped carbon dots (P-CDs) with pH sensitivity were coated on starch/polyvinyl alcohol film (SP film) by a simple scrape-coating method and applied to monitor the freshness of pork. The P-CDs@SP film exhibits a uniform surface, excellent mechanical properties, higher light blocking. When the total volatile base nitrogen (TVB-N) of pork exceeded 15 mg/100 g, the color of the film changed from brownish-yellow to purple-red, and the total color difference (ΔE) of the film obviously increased from 10.24 to 9.37 to 20.75 and 22.93 under the storage conditions of 25 and 4 °C, respectively. This can be attributed to the abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of P-CDs and SP film to promote their stable binding through hydrogen bonds, which greatly improves the hydrophilicity of the films, providing conditions for the rapid conversion of the TVB-N to ammonia and the formation of an alkaline environment. This work can simply and effectively monitor the freshness of pork through the changes of film color, will provide a new idea for the preparation of biodegradable smart packaging materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Meat is an indispensable food in human life. The proteins of meat are easily decomposed by microorganisms and spoilage. When the meat is spoiled, it produces large amounts of total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N), which increase the pH inside the package (the range of change is approximately 5.8–7) (Cheng et al., 2022; Zhang, Zou, et al., 2022). Spoiled meat not only costs businesses and customers money, it can also cause serious harm to human health (Hernández-García et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2024). Therefore, it is of great significance to monitor the freshness of the meat. Smart packaging is a new type of packaging that could monitor the quality change status of packaged food and provide real-time information to consumers, which is the current research frontier and hot spot of food packaging (Ghaani et al., 2016). In recent years, several smart-packages have been developed for monitoring the freshness of the meat, including pH-indicating packages (Anugrah et al., 2023), time-temperature-indicating packages (Liu, Cheah, et al., 2024), and radio-frequency identification tags (Iaccheri et al., 2022). But these smart-packages still suffer from some disadvantages in practical use. Firstly, most smart packages carry extra sensors to monitor the temperature, humidity, and atmosphere inside the package, etc. These sensors are complex and costly to use, which is not convenient for customers to recognize in their daily lives (Lee et al., 2019). Secondly, most of the freshness indicator packages add some environmentally responsive dye substances such as bromophenol blue (Dirpan & Hidayat, 2023), anthocyanins (Liu et al., 2023), curcumin (Lin et al., 2024), etc. These dyes have certain contact safety hazards and high extraction costs (Huang et al., 2024). Thirdly, most of the smart-packages are still based on petroleum-based materials, which are difficult to degrade and would cause harm to the environment when discarded. Therefore, convenience, environmental, and safety will become the new goal of intelligent packaging research (Tavassoli et al., 2021).

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon nanomaterials, and are characterized by low cost, low toxicity, and excellent physicochemical properties (Liu, Zhong, et al., 2024; Shi et al., 2023). CDs have been widely used in tumor therapy, biosensing, and fluorescent probes. Most CDs are pH-sensitive due to the protonated and deprotonated, which in turn changed the optical properties of CDs (Zhang et al., 2019). As an n-type donor, phosphorus-doping is beneficial to form substitution defects in the carbon clusters, giving more active sites to pH (Zhang, Li, et al., 2023). Therefore, P-CDs could produce indications via pH changes induced by changes in food quality and could be effectively utilized in the monitoring of food freshness. However, most CDs-based sensors are used in the solution, which is not conducive to meat freshness monitoring. Load on the packaging material is a comment strategy for CDs to achieve the indication function. To load CDs on packaging material, some studies have been conducted on mixing CDs with film-formable raw materials by casting method (Si et al., 2022; Tao et al., 2022; Yan et al., 2021). However, this preparation method is easy to cause environmental pollution and increase production cost, and the indication of CDs in the matrix will be affected. Some studies have loaded CDs directly onto film substrates. For example, Das et al. (2021) hammer at coating CDs on the surface of polypropylene (PP) film by photochemical covalent grafting, while the hydrophobic surface of PP film makes it difficult to combine with the hydrophilic CDs. Therefore, it is necessary to find a film material that is environmentally friendly, easy to load with CDs, and does not interfere with its indication properties.

As a class of hydrophilic films, starch/polyvinyl alcohol film (SP film) has the advantages of good biocompatibility and biodegradability. SP film is a candidate in food packaging, preservation of freshness, and intelligent color development (Abedi-Firoozjah et al., 2023; Mary et al., 2022). SP film can be prepared by extrusion blow molding, a method that brings enhanced mechanical properties and a more environmentally friendly production environment (Cheng et al., 2021). SP film contains a large number of hydrophilic groups, which can effectively prevent the condensation of water droplets on the surface of the film when it is used as food packaging, thus preventing the foodstuffs from being contaminated by water droplets (Min et al., 2020). At the same time, the large number of hydrophilic groups also allows the film to be combined with a variety of modified additives to increase its application functionality. For example, Ortega et al. (2017) added silver nanoparticles to SP film by casting that can effectively inhibit the growth of bacteria. SP film is easier and faster to modify by surface coating or spraying. For example, Jayasekara et al. (2004) chose SP film as the substrate and surface coated it with a 1% chitosan/acetic acid solution to increase the hydrophobicity of the film. It can be speculated that the SP film easily combines with pH-sensitive CDs, providing a promising biodegradable material for the field of smart packaging of meat. In addition, the abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of SP film and CDs enhance the hydrophilicity of the film, thereby improving the ammonia sensitivity of the film and achieving efficient and sensitive real-time monitoring of meat freshness.

Motivated by these issues, first, SP film was prepared using mechanically activated pretreatment and extrusion-blowing methods (Wu, Li, et al., 2024). SP film has excellent mechanical properties, and good water vapor permeability, which makes them a high-performance biodegradable film. Subsequently, the P-doped carbon dots loaded starch/PVA film (P-CDs@SP film) was successfully prepared using a simple scrape-coating method for pork freshness monitoring. Herein, phytic acid, a natural raw material rich in phosphate groups, was utilized as the carbon and phosphorus source for the preparation of pH-sensitive P-CDs. The structural characteristics and bonding mechanism of the P-CDs and SP film were analyzed by FT-IR, XRD, and XPS measurements. The mechanical and physical properties of the films before and after loading the P-CDs were evaluated. The pH sensitivity of the P-CDs@SP film in different pH buffer solutions and different concentrations of ammonia atmosphere was investigated. The performance of the P-CDs@SP film for pork freshness monitoring was verified by pork packaging experiments at 25 and 4 °C.

Phytic acid solution (mass fraction: 50%) was purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cassava starch (ST) (total starch content in the dry basis: 98.2%; moisture content: 12.6%–13.8%; amylose content: 27%) was supplied by Guangxi State Farms Mingyang Starch Development Co., Ltd. (Nanning, China). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) ([−CH2CHOH−] n ; degree of polymerization: 1700; degree of alcoholysis: 99.0%; molecular weight: 73,900–82700) was supplied by Shanghai Qichen Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Jinan, China). Calcium acetate (CA) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Boric acid was purchased from Chengdu Cologne Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Glycerol, Sodium chloride potassium bicarbonate, dipotassium hydrogen phosphate and potassium dihydrogen phosphate were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). LB Nutrient Agar was purchased from Qingdao Hope Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, China). Ammonia was obtained from Guangdong Guanghua Science and Technology Co., Ltd. (Shantou, China). Fresh pork was obtained from the local vegetable market. All chemicals used in the study were of analytical grade.

P-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method based on previously reported methods (Srivastava et al., 2020), with several modifications. Briefly, 3 mL of phytic acid was mixed with 17 mL of deionized water thoroughly. Then the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and hydrothermally heated at 220 °C for 10 h. When the reaction was finished, cooled down to room temperature at atmospheric pressure. The product was centrifuged at 3100 × g speed at 25 °C for 5 min to remove the larger solids, and the supernatant was filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain a brown solution. The obtained brown solution was dialyzed for 24 h using dialysis bag which has a nominal cut-off of 1000 Da to obtain a pure solution of P-CDs. The solution was dried and diluted with 50 mL of deionized water, and stored at 4 °C for a maximum of 2 weeks for further use.

SP film was prepared according to our previous methods (Wu, Li, et al., 2024). Briefly, a mixture of ST, PVA and CA was obtained by mechanical activation, followed by the addition of a certain amount of glycerol, and SP film were prepared using a twin-screw extruder and a blown film extruder. The indicator P-CDs@SP film was prepared by a one-step scrape-coating method according to Das et al. (2021), with some modifications. In brief, 20 mL of P-CDs solution was mixed homogeneously with 0.2 g of glycerol. Afterward, SP films were cut into rectangles (15 × 25 cm2), then wetted and placed on the coating machine (MSK-AFA-HC100, Shenzhen Kejing Star Technology Co., Ltd., Shenzhen, China). The P-CDs and homogeneously mixed solution were coated to the one side of the SP film using a coating machine carefully, dried at 45 °C, and then removed and set aside. Finally, the obtained P-CDs@SP films were placed in a close environment with relatively humidity of 48% ± 2% and 25 ± 2 °C at least 48 h for equilibration before the following characterization and evaluation.

The functional groups of ST and PVA were obtained by a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) (IRTracer-100, SHIMADZU, Tokyo, Japan) in the wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm−1 and each spectrum represented an average of 32 consecutive scans at 2 cm−1 resolution. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of ST and PVA were obtained using an X-ray diffractometer (D/MAX 2500 V, Rigaku Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) in the 2θ range of 5°–80°, with a rate of 10°/min, an accelerating voltage of 150 kV, and a current of 40 mA.

The absorbance spectrum of P-CDs was measured using UV–Visible double beam spectrometer model (UH5300, HITACHI, Tokyo, Japan) in the scan range 200–900 nm. Fluorescence spectra of P-CDs were analyzed using a PL spectrophotometer (RF–5301 PC, Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan) operated at 200–600 nm range of excitation wavelength. The morphology of P-CDs was acquired on a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (TECNAI G2 F30, FEI, Hillsboro, USA). The functional groups of P-CDs were obtained by FT-IR. The elemental composition of P-CDs was determined by X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) (K-Alpha, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, USA) using Al Kα excitation radiation (1253.6 eV). XRD patterns of P-CDs were obtained using X-ray diffractometer. The zeta potential of P-CDs was determined using a zeta potential analyzer (ZS90, NANO, Malvern, UK).

The functional groups of the films were measured by attenuated total reflection-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) (IRTracer-100, SHIMADZU, Tokyo, Japan). The elemental composition of the films was determined by XPS. XRD patterns of the films were obtained using X-ray diffractometer. The surface morphology and cross-section morphology of the films were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (sigma 300, Carl Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany), and the micrographs were collected at accelerating voltage of 3 kV with a magnification of 5 k.
The light transmittance of the films was measured between a wavelength of 200–800 nm wavelength using UV–Visible double beam spectrometer model UH5300 (HITACHI, Tokyo, Japan) (Liu et al., 2018).

Tensile properties such as tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break % (EB) of the films were measured according to the standard method of ASTM method D882-09 using Instron Universal Testing Machine (Model 5565, Instron Engineering Corporation, Massachusetts, USA) (Song et al., 2024). The film samples were cut into rectangular strips (30 × 10 mm2), and the testing machine was operated in tensile mode with an initial grip separation of 200 mm and crosshead speed of 50 mm/min. Thickness of the films were determined using a micrometer (Dial thickness gauge 7301, Mitutoyo, Kawasaki, Japan) at an accuracy of 0.01 mm.

The water vapor permeability (WVP) of the films were measured using a Moisture permeability tester (PERMATRAN-W3/61, MOCON, Minnesota, USA) according to the ASTM E96-95 standard method (Chen et al., 2023). The films were cut into strips (20 × 20 mm2) and placed on top of a weighted cup containing deionized water, and the sealed WVP cups were placed in a humidity chamber so that water vapor only passed through the film. The temperature of the chamber was 25 °C and the humidity was 50% RH. The weight of the cup was recorded at 10 h intervals. The water vapor transmission rate (WVTR, g m−2 s) of the films was determined from the slope of the weight change versus time curve of the WVP cups. WVP (g m−1 Pa−1 s−1) was calculated as follows:
(1)

Where D denotes the thickness of the test membrane (mm), ΔP denotes the difference in water vapor pressure across the test membrane, S is the saturated water vapor pressure (Pa) under the experimental conditions, RH 1 and RH 2 denote the relative humidity inside and outside the cups, respectively.

The water contact angles (WCA) of the film samples were measured using a WCA analyzer (SDC-350W, SINDIN, Dongguan, China) (Chen, Liu, et al., 2020). The film samples (30 × 10 mm2) were placed on a horizontal Teflon-coated steel stage on the analyzer. A drop of distilled water (∼10 μL) was dropped on the film surface using a micro-syringe, and the WCA was measured immediately.

The moisture content (MC) of the films was measured by weight loss method (Yong et al., 2019). The films were equilibrated at 23 °C, 50% RH for 72 h, cut into squares (20 × 20 mm2) and weighed (W 0). The samples were dried in an oven for 24 h until a constant weight (W 1) was reached. Three measurements were repeated. The MC (%) of the films were calculated as follows:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color response of P-CDs and P-CDs@SP film
ce:section.ce:para: The color change of P-CDs solutions and P-CDs@SP film in phosphate buffer solutions of different pH were determined. After mixing 1 mL of P-CDs solution with 4 mL of buffer solution in different pH (pH = 4–9), UV absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra were measured using a UV spectrophotometer and a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The P-CDs@SP films (30 × 30 mm2) were immersed into the buffer solution (pH = 4–9) for 5 min, and then excess water was removed from the surface of the films. The colorimetric values of the films were obtained using the Color recognition color picker software (V1.6, Xi'an Banligeban Software Co., Ltd., Xi'an, China) in the smartphone (Xiao et al., 2020).

Ammonia gas response experiments were further carried out to evaluate the color change sensitivity of the as-prepared P-CDs@SP film. Alkaline atmosphere was simulated using ammonia at different concentrations (0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, and 0.25 mg/mL). The side of the P-CDs@SP film coated with P-CDs was placed approximately 20 mm above a conical flask containing ammonia and allowed to stand at 25 °C for 0–10 min. After that the films were photographed and the colorimetric values of the films were obtained using the color reorganization software in the smartphone (Liu et al., 2022).

Color parameters including L value (lightness), a value (redness-greenness), and b value (yellow-blue) of films were measured by using a smartphone software “Color recognition color picker” with a white color plate as a standard background. The total color difference (ΔE) was calculated as follows:
(3)
Δ E =

Where L, a, and b are the chromaticity values of the film at the time of sampling; L 0, a 0, and b 0 are the chromaticity values of the film at 0 min (Chen, Zhang, et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the stability of the films
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the stability of P-CDs film, the films were placed at 25 °C, 50% RH and exposed to air for 30 days (Qin et al., 2021). Then the pH-sensitivity of the films were evaluated using the Color recognition color picker software.

Wrapping a few pieces of fresh pork with the P-CDs coated side of a P-CDs@SP film. Several pieces of fresh pork (20.00 g) were wrapped in P-CDs@SP film and heat-sealed by sealing machine. The packaged samples were placed in a constant temperature and humidity chamber at 25 °C, 50% RH and 4 °C, 50% RH, and the color change of the film was observed. The TVB-N of pork was determined every 12 h according to the method of GB 5009. 228–2016. Briefly, 10.00 g of pork sample was stirred and added to 100 mL of deionized water, mixed thoroughly, and then 5 mL of 1% potassium bicarbonate was added. Finally, TVB-N was determined using Kjeltec Auto Analyzer (K-375, BUCHI, Zurich, Switzerland), where a boric acid solution of pH = 4.65 was used as absorbent and titrated using 0.10 mol/L HCl solution. The total viable counts (TVC) of pork were determined every 12 h according to the method of GB 4789. 2–2022. Briefly, about 5.00 g of pork was added to 45 mL of 0.85% NaCl solution and mixed well. The mixture was serially diluted tenfold with 0.85% NaCl, and 200 μL of the dilution was spread on agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for 48 h.

All experiments were carried out at least in triplicate. Duncan test and one-way analysis of variance were used for multiple comparisons by the SPSS 13.0 software package. Differences were considered statistically significant if p < 0.05.

The morphology and size distribution of P-CDs were analyzed using TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the P-CDs revealed a distinct spherical shape and were dispersed uniformly. By counting the size of 100 pieces P-CDs in TEM image, it could be seen that the particle sizes of P-CDs were concentrated in the range of 2–3 nm (Fig. 1b), less than 10 nm, was within the quantum range (Song et al., 2022). The absence of visible lattice in the TEM image suggested that P-CDs were amorphous in nature, which was further confirmed by the broad peak at 2θ = 21.8° in the XRD pattern of P-CDs (Liao et al., 2021) (Fig. 1c).

The FT-IR spectrum of P-CDs was shown in Fig. 1d. The peaks at 2981 and 1651 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C–H and CC in P-CDs, which were the main component of the carbon network skeleton (Khan et al., 2023a). Phytic acid decomposed into phosphoric acid and inositol fragments at high pressure and temperature, and the fragments subsequently recombined to form the carbon network skeleton (Xing et al., 2023). The presence of inositol led to the formation of a large number of hydroxyl groups on the surface of the carbon network, which was confirmed by the stretching vibration of O–H at 3421 cm−1 (Liao et al., 2020). The obvious peaks at 1087 and 1049 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of PO and P–O–C, and the peak at 540 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of P–O–H (Xing et al., 2023), which indicated the attachment of phosphate-oxygen groups on the carbon skeleton. The FT-IR spectrum confirmed that the skeleton network of P-CDs was composed of sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbons and was attached with abundant hydroxyl and phosphorus oxygen groups.

XPS analysis showed that the as-prepared P-CDs were mainly composed of C, O, and P elements. The three significant signals at 133, 284, and 532 eV (Fig. 1e) can be assigned to P 2p, C 1s, and O 1s, and their contents were estimated to be 0.42%, 76.34%, and 23.24%, respectively. According to Fig. 1f‒h, the high resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, O 1s, and P 2p could be fitted to CC (283.8 eV), C–C/C–H (284.5 eV), C–O (285.6 eV), and CO (287.8 eV); C–O (530.6 eV), PO (532.0 eV), P–O (532.9 eV), and CO (534.9 eV) (Xing et al., 2023); P–O (132.8 eV) and PO (133.8 eV) (Fang et al., 2024), respectively. Both FT-IR and XPS results indicated that the surfaces of P-CDs were rich in hydroxyl and phosphate oxygen groups, which not only promised it good hydrophilicity but also gave it a condition for protonation and deprotonation.

Fluorescence (FL) excitation, emission, and UV–Visible absorption spectra of P-CDs were measured to investigate their optical properties. As shown in Fig. 2 a, P-CDs had a distinct absorption peak at 450 nm, which can be attributed to the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states in strong emission (Li et al., 2017; Zhi et al., 2018). The optimal excitation wavelength was located at 478 nm and the optimal emission band was obtained at 503 nm. This can be attributed to the n-π* electron leaps in CO and PO (Kilic et al., 2022). Inset of Fig. 2a showed that P-CDs solution exhibited brownish-yellow color under nature light and yellow-green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light.

Photographs of P-CDs in different pH solutions (4–9) under daylight were shown in Fig. 2b. At pH = 4–5, there was almost no color change can be observed. At pH = 6, the color of P-CDs changed to light pink, and at pH = 7–9, the color changed to purple and gradually deepened. Meanwhile, the fluorescence color of P-CDs solution also showed obvious pH-responsive. In the pH range of 4–9, the fluorescence of P-CDs gradually changed from yellow-green to blue (Fig. 2c). Fig. 2d showed that when pH increased from 4 to 9, the redshift of absorption peak from 450 to 495 nm can be observed. The colorimetric response of P-CDs to pH can be attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of phosphorus-oxygen groups on their surfaces, which led to the interruption and formation of π-π* electrons (Wang et al., 2017). Correspondingly, fluorescence emission spectra (ex = 450 nm) in Fig. 2e also showed that the emission peak blueshift from 504 nm (yellow-green fluorescence) to 450 nm (blue fluorescence) with the increasing of pH and the fluorescence intensity gradually increased. The blue shift of the emission peak could be attributed to the fluctuation of the Fermi energy level of the emission sites in P-CDs under alkaline conditions (Khan et al., 2023a). The surface zeta potential (Fig. 2f) showed that the zeta potential increased from −5.62 to −23.9 eV at pH = 4–9, and this result also confirmed the deprotonation of the P-CDs in alkaline solution (Su et al., 2019).

SEM images (Fig. 3 a and e) showed that the surfaces of both SP film and P-CDs@SP film were smooth, continuous, and dense, indicating that the loading of P-CDs did not have much effect on the morphology of SP film. In Fig. 3b and f, it can be seen that the particles on the surface of P-CDs@SP film were more pronounced, which could be attributed to the combination of P-CDs with the film surface and then aggregated into larger particles, thus increasing the roughness of the film surface (Sun et al., 2022). As can be seen in Fig. 3c and g, the cross-section of the P-CDs@SP film showed an increase in thickness to the cross-section of the SP film, which can be attributed to the introduction of the coating. Fig. 3d and h showed high-fold SEM images of the films cross-section, the pores in the cross-section of the P-CDs@SP film became more, which might be caused by the evaporation of water inside the film during the preparation process.

The functional groups and structural features of SP film and P-CDs@SP film were characterized using FT-IR (Fig. 3i). SP film has characteristic peaks at 3278 cm−1 (O–H stretching from ST and PVA), 2929 cm−1 (C–H stretching from alkyl group), 1647 cm−1 (bound water), 1330 cm−1 (C–O stretching from ST and PVA), and 1035 cm−1 (C–O–C stretching from ST) (Qin et al., 2021). For P-CDs@SP film, the peak at 1647 cm−1 of the film was shifted to 1651 cm−1, indicating hydrogen bonding interactions between SP film and P-CDs. There was not much difference in the FT-IR spectrum of synthesized P-CDs@SP film and SP film, only slight changes in the intensity and peak position could be observed in P-CDs@SP film. This may be due to the low content of P-CDs in the SP film, where the peaks of SP film covered the characteristic absorption peaks of P-CDs (Koshy et al., 2021), and the O–H, C–H, and C–O–C stretching vibrations overlap in almost the same infrared region.

Fig. 3m showed the XRD patterns of P-CDs@SP film and SP film. The characteristic diffraction peaks of P-CDs@SP film appeared at 2θ = 16.26°, 19.64°, and 22.2°, which were consistent with the diffraction peak positions of SP film (Petry et al., 2024). This indicated that the P-CDs covered the SP film uniformly and in small amounts. However, the broadening of the diffraction peak at 19.64° for P-CDs@SP film indicated that the crystallinity of the film was affected by the addition of P-CDs. This may be attributed to the hydrogen bonding between P-CDs and the SP film affecting the hydrogen bonding within the starch and PVA (Qin et al., 2021).

The elemental compositions of SP film and P-CDs@SP film were analyzed by XPS. The full scan XPS investigation (Fig. 3j) showed that the SP film was mainly composed of C and O elements. The two significant signals at 286 and 532 eV can be attributed to C 1s and O 1s, and their contents were estimated to be 69.14% and 30.16%, respectively. The full scan XPS investigation also showed that the P-CDs@SP film was mainly composed of P, C, and O elements. The three significant signals at 133, 284 and 532 eV can be attributed to P 2p, C 1s and O 1s, and their contents were estimated to be 2.76%, 68.47%, and 28.78%, respectively. The XPS C 1s spectra of P-CDs@SP film (Fig. 3o) can be fitted with four peaks corresponding to CC (283.8 eV), C–C/C–H (284.2), C–O (285.6 eV), and CO (287.5 eV) (Xing et al., 2023), respectively. Comparison of the XPS C 1s spectra of SP film (Fig. 3k) showed that the peaks of C–C/C–H, and C–O were negatively shifted, indicating that the addition of P affects the electron cloud density of C (Zhang et al., 2018). Fig. 3p showed that the high-resolution O 1s spectrum of P-CDs@SP film can be fitted to four peaks corresponding to C–O (530.6 eV), C–OH/PO (531.6 eV), C–O–C/P–O (532.4 eV), and CO (533.5 eV). Comparison of the XPS O 1s spectra of the SP film (Fig. 3l) showed that the incorporation of P-CDs brought phosphorus-oxygen groups to the film, which negatively shifted the peaks of C–OH/PO and C–O–C/P–O (Khan et al., 2023b). Fig. 3n shows that the P 2p spectra of P-CDs@SP film can be fitted with two different peaks corresponding to P–O (133.0 eV) and PO (134.0 eV). By comparing the elemental compositions of the SP film, it could be seen that the P-CDs@SP film was successfully loaded with P-CDs, and phosphorus-oxygen functional groups were also present on the surface of the film. The results of XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy confirmed that the coatings have not altered the basic structure of the P-CDs, and that large number of hydroxyl groups were still present on the surface of the film, which provided a structural basis for the film in colorimetric applications.

The mechanical and physical properties of SP film and P-CDs@SP film, including TS, EB, WVP, MC, and WCA, were shown in Table 1 . Though the loading of P-CDs had a certain impact on the mechanical and physical properties of SP film, the as-prepared P-CDs@SP film still met the requirements of food packaging. The thickness of the P-CDs@SP film was 47 μm, which was an increase of about 10 μm over the SP film. It was also consistent with the results shown in the SEM images of the film sections. The reason for the increase in thickness could be attributed to the successful load of P-CDs coating. The TS of P-CDs@SP film was 26.80 MPa and it was significantly decreased compared to the SP film. The EB of P-CDs@SP film was 257.54%, which significantly elevated compared to the SP film. The addition of P-CDs and glycerol affected the hydrogen bonds inside the SP film, which reduced the intermolecular force between starch and PVA, resulting in the decrease of TS. At the same time, the addition of P-CDs increased the fluidity of SP starch and PVA molecules in the starch film, which made the film have higher ductility and led to the increase of EB (Miao et al., 2021). These showed that the P-CDs@SP film had good mechanical properties and had outstanding advantages in food packaging. The WVP of P-CDs@SP film was 9.21 × 10−13 g m−1 s−1 Pa−1, which showed a significantly increase compared to the SP film (Gomes et al., 2022; Min et al., 2023). These results suggested that the P-CDs@SP film had a good water barrier, which was favorable for the film to be used in food packaging.

It is worth to be mentioned that the addition of P-CDs led to a slight increase in the MC of the film and a slight decrease in the WCA, but these changes were not significant. This was partly because the P-CDs and glycerol brought more hydroxyl groups to the surface of the film, and partly because some of the water was lost during the preparation of the film, which together affected the MC and WCA of the film (Yong et al., 2019). Because TVB-N can only cause pH to rise if dissolved in water, the increase of hydrophilicity and moisture content of the film induced by the loading of P-CDs created a favorable hydrophilic environment in response to TVB-N during packaging.

As shown in Fig. 4 , the incorporation of P-CDs significantly reduced the transmittance of the films, and the transmittance of the P-CDs@SP film in the UV range of 200–300 nm was less than 10. The addition of P-CDs caused refraction and scattering of the light (Qin et al., 2021), and thus the P-CDs@SP film had a certain ability to block UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Color responses and stability of P-CDs@SP film
ce:section.ce:para: The color response of P-CDs@SP film to different pH was shown in Fig. 5 a, where the color change of the films was observed in pH = 4–9 buffer solution. The P-CDs@SP film showed brownish-yellow color at pH = 4 and gradually changed to light red, light purple, and purplish-red when pH ranged from 5 to 9. As shown in Fig. 5b, with pH value increase from 4 to 9, L value of the P-CDs@SP film decreased gradually, and only slightly increase for a value can be observed, while b value decreased gradually. The ΔE value of the P-CDs@SP film showed a gradient increase from 8.06 to 42.20 with pH value increasing from 4 to 9, which was consistent with the color change in the photographs, allowing a visualize the chromaticity method of the film for pH value monitoring. Also, the fast and stable pH response process of the P-CDs@SP film confirmed that the SP film had almost no effect on the colorimetric performance of P-CDs. This due to P-CDs loaded onto the SP film surface were still rich in phosphorus-oxygen groups, and these phosphorus-oxygen groups can be deprotonated under the action of OH−, which resulted in a color change of P-CDs@SP film. These results suggested that P-CDs@SP film had the potential application in monitoring the freshness of food.

To evaluate the stability of the P-CDs@SP film, the films were placed in air at a certain temperature and humidity for 30 days. As can be seen in Fig. 5b, the P-CDs@SP film still showed a brownish-yellow to purplish-red color change at pH = 4–9. Fig. 5e showed that the ΔE changed from 8.10 to 34.34 at pH = 4–9, which provided the pH response function of the film was recognizable to the naked-eye. As a result, P-CDs@SP film maintained a stable pH response after 30 days of storage.

Meat rich in protein and usually produces large amounts of TVB-N compounds during the spoilage process, which results in an alkaline environment inside the package (Lin et al., 2022). Therefore, the sensitivity of the P-CDs@SP film under alkaline conditions was a key factor for food freshness monitoring. Fig. 5c showed the variation of color of P-CDs@SP film under different ammonia concentrations. Where the concentrations of ammonia were 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, and 0.25 mg/mL. After exposure to ammonia for 5 min, the color of the P-CDs@SP film changed from brownish-yellow to purplish-red with the increase of ammonia concentration. The color of the prepared P-CDs@SP film almost completely changed to purple after 10 min in all the studied concentrations of ammonia, and the color of the film gradually deepened as the concentration of ammonia increased. This reflected the fast response of P-CDs@SP film to ammonia and the color change can be distinguished by the naked-eye. Fig. 5f and g showed the variation of colorimetric values of P-CDs@SP film in response to ammonia. The ΔE values of P-CDs@SP film increased with the increase of ammonia concentration from 0.05 to 0.25 mg/mL, regardless of the exposure to different ammonia concentrations for 5 or 10 min. The trends of ΔE, a, b, and L of P-CDs@SP film were like those in different pH solutions. In particular, the ΔE values showed a good linear relationship with ammonia in the concentration range of 0.05–0.25 mg/mL of ammonia water under the experimental conditions of 5th min and 10th min. The R
2 values between the ΔE and ammonia concentration were 0.9763 (5th min) and 0.9699 (10th min), respectively. This demonstrated that P-CDs@SP film can monitor the freshness of pork while also quantifying to some extent the change in ammonia within the package through the color difference value, which offered great application value in the field of smart packaging.

The color changes of P-CDs@SP film were more obvious under ammonia atmospheres, which indicated that the film also has a high pH sensitivity to the gas. This can be attributed to the fact that the SP film can effectively admit water molecules in the air as hydrophilic carriers. When ammonia reached the P-CDs@SP film, the water molecules on the surface of the film immediately dissociate OH− by water merging with ammonia, which increased the pH value of the surface of the P-CDs@SP film and promoted the deprotonation of P-CDs (Zhang, Sun, et al., 2023). Therefore, the color change of P-CDs@SP film in ammonia environment more truly reflected the colorimetric function of the film, and thus had good potential for application in TVB-N concentration for meat freshness monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of P-CDs@SP film in monitoring pork freshness
ce:section.ce:para: According to the Chinese national standard of GB/T 9959. 2–2008, TVB-N and TVC concentration was a key factor to evaluate the freshness of the meat. The meat was considered to be fresh when its TVB-N value was less than 15 mg/100 g; meat was considered sub-fresh when its TVB-N value was in the range of 15–20 mg/100 g; and meat was completely spoiled when its TVB-N value was greater than 20 mg/100 g (Karaca et al., 2023). At the same time, the TVC of fresh meat should be less than 6 log10 CFU/g. The performance of P-CDs@SP film as indicator of freshness was investigated by packaging pork in P-CDs@SP film at the storage temperature of 25 and 4 °C for 0–48 h (Fig. 6 a). It can be easily seen from Fig. 6a that the film color change was significant and highly consistent with the process of pork spoilage. At the same time the corresponding TVB-N and TVC concentrations were tested, and illustrated in Fig. 6b‒e.

The color change of the P-CDs@SP film was obvious after 24 h of storing at 25 °C and 48 h of storing at 4 °C, changing from brownish-yellow to purplish-red, the colorimetric values ΔE, a, b, and L of the P-CDs@SP film for pork packing were shown in Fig. 6f and g. The ΔE values increased from 10.24 to 9.37 to 20.75 and 22.93, respectively, which could be easily recognized by the naked-eye. At this time, the corresponding TVB-N concentrations of pork in the packages were 15.46 and 16.47 mg/100 g (Fig. 6b and d), respectively, the TVC concentration were 6.21 and 5.73 log10 CFU/g, respectively (Fig. 6c and e) (Wu, Liu, et al., 2024). These results indicated that P-CDs@SP film could send an early warning signal before the pork was about to spoil at different storage temperatures.

Packaging experiments confirmed that the intrinsic quality changes of pork during storage could be directly reflected on the as-prepared P-CDs@SP film. Changes in the ΔE value indicated that the trend of the colorimetric response of the P-CDs@SP film to pork freshness was the same as the trend of the colorimetric response of the film to ammonia-simulated conditions. Therefore, the as-prepared P-CDs@SP film had great potential for application in real-time monitoring of pork freshness by naked-eye.

The color change experiments in different and to find out the color change mechanism, the FT-IR spectra of P-CDs and P-CDs@SP film before and after introduction of ammonia (Fig. 6h and i) were performed. Fig. 6h showed that large number of hydroxyl and phosphate groups and intramolecular hydrogen bonds can be observed on the surface of P-CDs. With the introduction of ammonia (alkaline environments), the hydroxyl peaks of the P-CDs were widened, and the intensity of the P–O–H peak was weakened, indicating the dissociation of P–O–H (Su et al., 2019). The phosphate groups on the surface of P-CDs under alkaline conditions underwent deprotonation, leading to the dissociation of the intramolecular hydrogen bonds of P-CDs and thus changing the color of P-CDs. The FT-IR spectra of the P-CDs@SP film before and after ammonia environment were shown in Fig. 6i. The hydroxyl peak on the surface of the film after the color change was broadened and weakened in intensity, and the intensity of the peak of bound water was also slightly reduced. These results likewise confirmed the interaction of water molecules with ammonia on the P-CDs@SP film surface (Yang et al., 2023).

Therefore, the colorimetric pork freshness indicating mechanism of the as-prepared P-CDs@SP film can be ascribed as Fig. 7 . The large number of hydroxyl groups on the surface of the P-CDs@SP film gave the film a high degree of hydrophilicity and water storage during the packaging of pork. As TVB-N in the package continued to accumulate and reach the P-CDs@SP film surface, they hydrated with water molecules on the film surface to create an alkaline environment (Wu, Liao, et al., 2024). The alkaline conditions induced deprotonation of the P-CDs, which changed the color of the P-CDs@SP film. On this basis, the P-CDs@SP film enabled fast, non-destructive, naked-eye identification of the freshness of pork inside the package (Zhang, Huang, et al., 2022).

In conclusion, pH-sensitive P-CDs were prepared by the simple hydrothermal treatment of phytic acid. The as-prepared P-CDs showed a sensitive color change from brownish-yellow to purplish-red with increasing pH from 4 to 9 through the deprotonation of phosphate groups. SP film prepared by extrusion-blowing method were used as the substrate, and P-CDs were coated onto SP film by a simple coating method to prepare P-CDs@SP film. The P-CDs@SP film had excellent mechanical properties, with a TS of 26.80 MPa and an EB of 257.54%. After coating P-CDs, the WVP of the film increased from 6.68 × 10−13 to 9.21 × 10−13 g m−1 s−1 Pa−1. The film showed a consistent color change with P-CDs in different pH buffer solutions and ammonia atmospheres. Characterization results showed that P-CDs were loaded onto SP film by hydrogen bonding between hydroxyl groups, suggesting the loading on SP film had almost no effect on the pH responsive ability of P-CDs. The rich hydroxyl groups of P-CDs@SP film enabled it a good water storage performance, which encouraged ammonia to rapidly form an alkaline environment on the film surface. Pork packaging experiments confirmed that P-CDs@SP film changed color from brownish-yellow to purplish-red when the TVB-N of pork exceeded 15 mg/100 g. Taking advantage of convenience in preparation, rapid response to TVB-N, and biodegradable, the P-CDs@SP film can meet the demand for real-time, naked-eye monitoring of meat freshness and has great potential for widespread use in the field of smart packaging.

Yanjuan Zhang: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Tao Gan: Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation.
